project_overview.project_name: Water Security in the Dry Corridor of Honduras
project_overview.project_development_objective: To improve water service delivery and strengthen water governance in selected areas of the Dry Corridor of Honduras
project_overview.total_financing: US$85 million (US$70 million IDA credit + US$15 million Government counterpart)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Approximately 167,100 direct beneficiaries including rural farmers, small-town residents, and urban populations in the Dry Corridor; approximately 7,660 indirect beneficiary families

primary_locations: Municipalities: San Antonio de Flores, Pespire, San Lorenzo, Nacaome, La Venta, Curarén, La Paz, Manazapa
geographic_scope.coverage_type: Regional (Dry Corridor of Honduras with focus on Nacaome Basin as pilot)
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: Partial - The Dry Corridor is characterized by high poverty (76.7% poverty rate, 64.5% extreme poverty), water scarcity, climate vulnerability, and social tensions around water access. Honduras has experienced political instability and security challenges, though the project areas are not designated as active conflict zones. Water user conflicts are a significant risk.

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Strengthening Institutional Capacity for Water Resources Governance and Management
key_components.description: Strengthen hydrometeorological network, water resources information system (WRIS), and capacity development in Nacaome Basin; develop approaches for transparent water resources management and governance at local, basin, and national levels; support establishment of National Water Authority (ADA)

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Scaling up Resilient Hydraulic Infrastructure for Water Security
key_components.description: Sub-component 2.1: Design, construction, and establishment of integrated water security systems (SIAS) for water harvesting and storage in rural areas; training on O&M and agriculture; provision of sanitation units; watershed protection. Sub-component 2.2: Maximize benefits of José Cecilio del Valle (JCV) Dam through installation of spillway gates, construction of centralized water treatment plant (CWTP), dam safety improvements, and institutional arrangements for sustainable operation
key_components.financing: US$55.0 million IDA + US$15.0 million Government counterpart (total US$70 million)

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Project Management and Capacity Building
key_components.description: Project coordination and management through PMU; monitoring, evaluation, and impact assessment; fiduciary administration and audits; environmental and social risk management; stakeholder engagement and grievance management; gender strategy implementation; training of PMU staff
Provides immediate response to eligible emergencies through reallocation of project funds if an eligible crisis or emergency occurs

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Honduran Strategic Investment Office (INVEST-H) - a government agency under the General Government Coordination Ministry. INVEST-H will host the Project Management Unit (PMU) and coordinate with Ministry of Natural Resources and Environment (MIAMBIENTE), Ministry of Disaster Risk Management and National Contingencies (COPECO), National Institute of Forest Conservation (ICF), Ministry of Infrastructure and Public Services (INSEP), Ministry of Agriculture (SAG), and local water/irrigation organizations
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Inter-institutional Agreements to be signed between INVEST-H and: (1) MIAMBIENTE and COPECO for water resources information systems; (2) ICF for watershed management; (3) Municipalities and water/irrigation boards for each SIAS; (4) MIAMBIENTE for JCV Dam coordination. Agreements will clarify roles, responsibilities, and ensure cooperation. Water Basin Board and Drought Committee for Nacaome Basin will be strengthened. Coordination with other development partners through Water Donor Roundtable (Mesa de Donantes de Agua)
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers (July 2016, revised November 2017 and August 2018). Project Procurement Strategy for Development (PPSD) prepared. Procurement methods include: International competitive bidding for major works and goods; Quality and cost-based selection for consulting services. Prior review required for contracts above mandatory thresholds. INVEST-H will hire additional procurement specialists and officers. All procurement procedures defined in Project Operational Manual (POM)
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: INVEST-H responsible for overall M&E. Management Information System (MIS) includes: financial management module, beneficiary monitoring module with geo-referencing, control panel for results framework indicators. Semi-annual and annual progress reports, baseline and target data, midterm review, and final evaluation. External audit by private firm following International Standards on Auditing. Quarterly environmental and social compliance reports. M&E indicators track water access, irrigation access, local organizations' capacity, water storage, dam safety, sanitation access, citizen satisfaction, gender participation, and grievance resolution

identified_risks.risk_description: Political polarization may delay project approval; political changes at federal and municipal levels may result in deviations from project objectives; weak institutions and political instability in Honduras
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Strong engagement with stakeholders on government's national development plans; multiple consultations with key agencies during preparation and ongoing; high-level political dialogue on importance of water resources management; establishment of management structure involving multiple actors; training on participatory decision-making; robust communications campaigns on project objectives and benefits

identified_risks.risk_description: Adverse weather conditions and lower international prices for agricultural products (especially coffee) could create large agricultural losses; political polarization may delay water and energy sector reforms; COVID-19 pandemic expected to weaken growth and fiscal accounts
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Government aims to strengthen fiscal sustainability through tax code reform and enhanced transparency of electric utility; large foreign exchange reserves (20% of GDP) available to mitigate shocks; authorities implementing strong containment measures and Economic Rescue Plan for COVID-19

identified_risks.risk_description: Continued delays in implementing 2009 Water Law could limit government capacity to effectively plan, manage, and protect water resources; lack of implementing regulations and institutional framework for National Water Authority (ADA)
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project has strong focus on supporting information-based policy and strategic planning for effective water resources management; INVEST-H will provide technical assistance to support ministerial endorsement of by-law for ADA establishment; operationalization of ADA is priority action under Honduras DRM Development Policy Credit (CAT-DDO)

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Multisectoral nature of project activities requires strong leadership and technical capacity; INVEST-H, while experienced with World Bank projects, must manage complex inter-institutional coordination; potential bottlenecks in decision-making due to INVEST-H's growing portfolio and small management team
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Inter-institutional Agreements with clear delineation of roles and responsibilities; establishment of citizen engagement mechanisms; facilitation of expert involvement in areas with limited Honduras experience; provision of O&M training for beneficiaries for two years beyond project life; adequate staffing of PMU with qualified experts; clear delegation of decision-making authority to PMU coordinator; detailed specification of roles in POM

identified_risks.risk_description: While INVEST-H is well-established with experience in externally financed projects, its current structure and workload plus additional project workload may present challenges; potential for inadequate financial management and procurement capacity
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Additional experienced fiduciary staff located within PMU to supplement existing staffing; clear implementation agreements for all parties in POM; Financial Management Assessment (FMA) conducted and found INVEST-H's capacity meets World Bank requirements; INVEST-H to hire additional procurement specialists and officers; external audit by private firm following international standards; semi-annual interim financial reports; review of audit reports and follow-up on recommendations

identified_risks.risk_description: High environmental risk due to scope of civil works (Component 2); potential permanent flooding of approximately 40 ha of secondary forest for JCV Dam and 1.7-4.2 ha for new SIAS; potential safety risks from dam and water harvesting structures; potential impacts on fish populations and downstream flows; construction-stage impacts including labor influx, GBV risks, occupational health and safety risks; high social risk due to potential water user conflicts in target areas; potential adverse impacts on Indigenous Peoples or vulnerable groups; land acquisition and resettlement impacts
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Procurement of high-quality consulting firms for technical, environmental, and social assessments; Environmental and Social Impact Assessments (ESIA) prepared for JCV Dam and SIAS; Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF), Indigenous Peoples Planning Framework (IPPF), and Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) developed and disclosed; Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP) prepared; comprehensive dam safety assessment during implementation; panel of dam safety experts; Environmental and Social Commitment Plan (ESCP); Labor Management Procedures; Community Health and Safety Plans; contractors required to maintain ESHS officers; multidisciplinary supervision teams on site; design of SIAS to separate water for drinking and irrigation; micro-measurement systems for water use monitoring; citizen engagement activities with emphasis on user participation; training on integrated water resources management; compensation programs for forest loss and livelihood impacts; watershed management and restoration programs; mangrove conservation program; GBV training and code of conduct for contractors; equal opportunity recruitment practices; gender training in workplace

identified_risks.risk_description: Potential resistance from users to monitoring and control of water abstraction and discharges; problems related to land tenure in project intervention areas; water usage may be source of social conflict if water sharing measures not properly implemented; Honduras has experienced challenges with water allocation among local population, farmers, and hydropower producers
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Training on adequate integrated water resources management (IWRM) for beneficiaries; citizen engagement activities and strong participatory process in design of infrastructure, institutions, plans, and tariffs; minimum two years of technical assistance to water users after construction, particularly on O&M and fee collection; Inter-institutional Agreements clarifying responsibilities; establishment of water basin committees and user associations; design of SIAS with separation of water uses; micro-measurement systems; participatory planning with community involvement

identified_risks.risk_description: Honduras has substantial vulnerability to extreme climate events; current overall adaptive capacity remains low; risk of droughts, floods, and other climate-related hazards affecting project sustainability
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project design includes resilient and climate-smart practices and technologies; integrated, ecosystem-based approach to water infrastructure investments and water governance; watershed management planning to protect soil and vegetation; reforestation and sustainable landscape management; improved water retention and storage through SIAS; increased hydropower generation reducing GHG emissions; project estimated to achieve net emissions reduction of -12,446 tCO2-eq per year

triggered_safeguard_policies: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts (ESS1)
Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management (ESS3)
Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement (ESS5)
Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources (ESS6)
Indigenous Peoples/Sub-Saharan African Historically Underserved Traditional Local Communities (ESS7)
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Project-level grievance redress mechanisms established through Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP). Communities and individuals can submit complaints through project-level GRM or World Bank's Grievance Redress Service (GRS). World Bank Inspection Panel available for complaints regarding World Bank non-compliance with policies. INVEST-H will establish and manage grievance management system with quarterly reporting to World Bank. Grievances and claims to be registered and addressed within agreed timeframes
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Gender assessment conducted identifying equity gaps in work, participation in water-related decision-making, and social/cultural expectations. Only 20% of community-based decision-making members are female; women rarely in leadership positions. Project aims to: (1) Enhance livelihoods and economic opportunities for women through training in soft skills (financial literacy, business management) and technical skills (water supply fitters, plumbers); (2) Foster women's participation in decision-making in watershed management through communication campaigns, workshops with women leaders, affirmative measures (minimum quotas for female participation and leadership), delinking land ownership from membership, adequate meeting timing/locations; (3) Promote female employment in infrastructure projects through equal opportunity recruitment, gender training, and appropriate working conditions; (4) Reduce female water-carrying burden through improved water access; (5) Support women's income generation through training on land rights, crop inputs, and financing options. Gender strategy and action plan to be designed during implementation. Gender indicators track: % female members in community-based decision-making structures, % in leadership roles, % female participants in irrigation activities with improved income
vulnerable_groups: Indigenous Peoples and Afro-descendants (estimated 8.6% of national population, with >70% living in poverty)
Female-headed households (estimated 25% of Dry Corridor households)
Subsistence farmers and landless farmers/day laborers
Rural populations (60% of rural population below poverty line in 2018)
Populations affected by water scarcity and drought
Populations at risk of displacement due to land acquisition for infrastructure

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: The project addresses water-related conflicts through: (1) Integrated water resources management approach promoting coordination among water users; (2) Establishment of water basin boards and drought committees to manage water allocation and resolve conflicts; (3) Participatory design of infrastructure and water-sharing arrangements; (4) Separation of water for drinking and irrigation purposes in SIAS design; (5) Micro-measurement systems to monitor water use; (6) Inter-institutional agreements clarifying roles and responsibilities; (7) Training on IWRM and participatory decision-making; (8) Citizen engagement mechanisms and grievance redress systems. Project recognizes Honduras has experienced challenges with water allocation among local population, farmers, and hydropower producers. Design specifically addresses potential water user conflicts in target areas through community participation and transparent governance mechanisms
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Honduras is characterized by: (1) High poverty rates (48.3% below national poverty line, 22.9% in extreme poverty); (2) High inequality (Gini 51.1 in 2016); (3) Severe climate vulnerability - second most severely affected country 1997-2016, losing 1.8% GDP/year to climatic events; (4) Weak institutions and political instability; (5) High exposure to droughts, floods, and other extreme weather; (6) Uncontrolled disease outbreaks (Dengue fever epidemic, COVID-19); (7) Limited economic diversification and weak private sector; (8) High crime and migration pressures; (9) Inadequate water resources management and governance; (10) Dry Corridor specifically: 76.7% poverty rate, 64.5% extreme poverty, subsistence agriculture dependent on rainfall, chronic child malnutrition (29% rural vs 15% urban). Project designed to build resilience to climate shocks and reduce vulnerability through improved water security, which is critical for food security, health, and reducing climate-driven migration
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Project includes security-related considerations: (1) Dam safety assessments and emergency preparedness plans for JCV Dam including dam break analysis, inundation maps, and evacuation procedures; (2) Occupational health and safety requirements for construction contractors; (3) Community health and safety plans addressing labor influx risks; (4) Gender-based violence (GBV) screening and mitigation measures including GBV training for contractors and communities, code of conduct with GBV provisions, monitoring of compliance; (5) Traffic safety measures for construction sites; (6) Measures to prevent exploitation and abuse during construction phase; (7) Grievance mechanisms for reporting security and safety concerns. Project does not operate in active conflict zones but recognizes Honduras' security challenges and includes safeguards to protect beneficiaries and workers
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Yes - Land acquisition and resettlement impacts identified: (1) Permanent flooding of approximately 40 hectares of secondary forest for JCV Dam reservoir level increase; (2) Flooding of 1.7-4.2 hectares for new SIAS water harvesting reservoirs; (3) Potential land acquisition for water treatment plant, pipelines, and other infrastructure; (4) Potential restrictions on land use in catchment areas. Mitigation measures: (1) Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) prepared and disclosed; (2) Resettlement Action Plans (RAPs) to be developed as needed during implementation; (3) Compensation programs for loss of forest cover and livelihood impacts; (4) Watershed management and restoration programs; (5) Community participation in site selection for SIAS to minimize displacement; (6) SIAS design requirement that systems not be in environmentally or socially sensitive areas and have strong community support; (7) Legal declarations of water source zones and watershed management plans; (8) Environmental services schemes to compensate upstream landowners for maintaining forest cover. Project preparation included extensive stakeholder engagement to identify and address displacement risks
